perturbation.original_text: Covenantor acknowledges that the Company and its employees and shareholders have over several years devoted substantial time, effort and resources to developing the Assets which consist of the Company's trade secrets and its other confidential and proprietary information as well as the Company's relationships with customers, suppliers, employees and others doing business with the Company; that such relationships, trade secrets and other information are vital to the successful conduct of the Company's business by Buyer in the future; that because of Covenantor's access to such confidential information and trade secrets, Covenantor would be in a unique position to divert business from Buyer and to commit irreparable damage to Buyer were Covenantor to be allowed to compete with Buyer or to commit any of the other acts prohibited below; that the enforcement of said restrictive covenants against Covenantor would not impose any undue burden upon Covenantor; that none of said restrictive covenants is unreasonable as to period or geographic area; and that the ability to enforce said restrictive covenants against Covenantor is a material inducement to the decision of Buyer to consummate the transactions contemplated in the Purchase Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Covenantor acknowledges that the Company and its employees and shareholders have seemingly devoted time, effort, and resources to developing the Assets which consist of the Company's trade secrets and its other confidential information as well as the Company's relationships with customers, suppliers, employees and others doing business with the Company; that such relationships, trade secrets and other information are possibly vital to the successful conduct of the Company's business by Buyer in the future; that Covenantor's access to such information might place them in a position to divert business from Buyer and potentially damage Buyer were Covenantor to compete with Buyer; that enforcing restrictive covenants against Covenantor might not impose an undue burden; that these restrictive covenants might be reasonable regarding period or geographic area; and that enforcing said restrictive covenants is a potential inducement for Buyer to finalize the Purchase Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text uses vague language such as 'seemingly devoted,' 'possibly vital,' 'might place them,' 'might not impose,' and 'potential inducement.' This introduces ambiguity regarding the certainty of the facts acknowledged by the Covenantor, weakening the enforceability of the non-compete and non-disclosure clauses. The original text states clear acknowledgement, which implies a binding agreement, but the modified text raises doubts about the validity and enforceability of the claims and the willingness of the Covenantor to be bound to these agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 16600 states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' The original contract aims to restrict the Covenantor's ability to compete. The modified text weakens the acknowledgement of key facts supporting the non-compete agreement, thereby increasing the likelihood that a court could find the agreement unenforceable under § 16600 because the acknowledgement is no longer clear and binding, thus the restraints appear less justified and more easily voided. If the initial acknowledgement is ambiguous, the basis for restricting trade becomes weaker.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

(b)(1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that weaken the non-compete agreement, potentially rendering it unenforceable under California Business and Professions Code § 16600. The original text uses definitive language, solidifying the Covenantor's acknowledgement of critical facts, which supports the enforceability of the restrictive covenants. The changed text weakens these acknowledgements with phrases like 'seemingly devoted,' 'possibly vital,' 'might place them,' 'might not impose,' and 'potential inducement,' thereby creating uncertainty about the factual basis upon which the non-compete agreement rests. As stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' By making the initial acknowledgements ambiguous, the basis for restricting trade becomes less justified and more easily voided, thus creating a contradiction. The 'Ambiguity Legal Contradiction' definition is also met, given that ambiguities were introduced.

perturbation.original_text: Covenantor shall not at any time within the period commencing on the date hereof and ending on the fourth (4/th/) anniversary of the date hereof (the "Restricted Period"), have any -2- <PAGE> ownership interest (of record or beneficial) in or have any interest as an employee, salesman, consultant, officer or director in, or otherwise aid or assist in any manner, any firm, corporation, limited liability company, partnership, proprietorship or other business that engages in any California county, or in any other state, country or geographic area, listed in Exhibit "A" hereof in a business that is similar to and directly or indirectly offers products or services in competition with the business in which the Company was engaged in such California county, state, country or other geographic area as of the Closing, so long as Buyer, or any successor in interest of Buyer to the business and goodwill of the Company, remains engaged in such business in any such California county, state, country or other geographic area or continues to solicit customers or potential customers therein
perturbation.changed_text: Covenantor shall endeavor, within the period commencing on the date hereof and ending on the fourth (4/th/) anniversary of the date hereof (the "Restricted Period"), to avoid having any -2- <PAGE> ownership interest (of record or beneficial) in or having any role as an employee, salesman, consultant, officer or director in, or assisting in a significant manner, any firm, corporation, limited liability company, partnership, proprietorship or other business that arguably engages in any California county, or in any other state, country or geographic area, listed in Exhibit "A" hereof in a business that is somewhat similar to and possibly offers products or services in competition with the business in which the Company was engaged in such California county, state, country or other geographic area as of the Closing, if Buyer, or any successor in interest of Buyer to the business and goodwill of the Company, conceivably remains engaged in such business in any such California county, state, country or other geographic area or intermittently continues to solicit customers or potential customers therein
perturbation.explanation: By replacing 'shall not' with 'shall endeavor to avoid', the obligation becomes non-mandatory. Also, the use of words like 'arguably engages', 'somewhat similar to', 'possibly offers', and 'conceivably remains engaged' creates ambiguity and weakens the restrictive covenant. This makes it difficult to determine when a breach has occurred, hindering enforcement. Finally, 'assisting in a significant manner' introduces a subjective element.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 16600 voids contracts restraining someone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. By making the non-compete obligation less strict and more ambiguous, the modified text increases the likelihood a court will find the restraint unreasonable and therefore void under § 16600. The added vagueness makes it harder to prove a violation, further undermining the enforceability and reasonableness of the restraint.

perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the original non-compete agreement, creating a contradiction with California Business and Professions Code § 16600, which generally voids contracts restraining someone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. The original text used definitive terms like 'shall not,' creating a clear prohibition. The changed text replaces this with weaker, ambiguous terms like 'shall endeavor to avoid,' 'arguably engages,' 'somewhat similar to,' 'possibly offers,' and 'conceivably remains engaged.' This increased ambiguity and discretionary language undermines the enforceability of the non-compete, making it more likely to be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade under § 16600. As stated in scraped law snippet 1, 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void,' with exceptions not applicable here. The changes in the modified text make it significantly more difficult to prove a violation, thereby contravening the spirit and intent of § 16600 to protect an individual's right to engage in their profession.

perturbation.original_text: During the Restricted Period, Covenantor shall not solicit or assist any other person to solicit any business (other than for Buyer) from any present or past customer of the Company; or request or advise any present or future customer of the Company to withdraw, curtail or cancel its business dealings with Buyer; or commit any other act or assist others to commit any other act which might injure the business of Buyer.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Restricted Period, Covenantor should generally refrain from directly soliciting any business (other than for Buyer) from current or former customers of the Company to a noticeable extent; nor should Covenantor actively encourage present or future customers of the Company to significantly withdraw from their business dealings with Buyer; nor should Covenantor intentionally commit any act that demonstrably harms the business of Buyer.
perturbation.explanation: The original text imposes a clear prohibition on soliciting, requesting, advising, or committing acts that injure the business. The modified version uses weaker and more ambiguous language such as 'should generally refrain,' 'to a noticeable extent,' 'actively encourage,' 'significantly withdraw,' and 'demonstrably harms.' This introduces subjectivity and makes it harder to prove a violation, weakening the protection afforded to the Buyer.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 16600 renders void contracts that restrain trade. The modified text, by weakening the restrictions on solicitation and other actions that could harm the business, makes it more likely that a court would find the remaining restraint unreasonable under § 16600. The vagueness and subjectivity make it more difficult for the Buyer to enforce the non-solicitation agreement, thereby making the restrictive covenant more susceptible to being deemed void.

(b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by using terms like 'should generally refrain,' 'to a noticeable extent,' 'actively encourage,' 'significantly withdraw,' and 'demonstrably harms.' This weakens the original restrictions on solicitation and actions harming the business. The original text provides a clearer standard, whereas the modified version weakens this standard through subjective terms. California Business and Professions Code § 16600 states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' By weakening the restrictions, the modified text increases the likelihood that a court would find the restraint unreasonable and therefore void under § 16600, because the ambiguity makes it difficult to enforce.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the termination of Covenantor's employment with Buyer, Covenantor shall immediately surrender to Buyer all notes, data, sketches, drawings, manuals, documents, records, data bases, programs, blueprints, memoranda, specifications, customer lists, financial reports, equipment and all other physical forms of expression incorporating or containing any Confidential Information, it being distinctly understood that all such writings, physical forms of expression and other things are the exclusive property of Buyer.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the termination of Covenantor's employment with Buyer, Covenantor should endeavor to return to Buyer all tangible items reasonably believed to contain Confidential Information to the best of their understanding, insofar as such items are readily accessible. While these physical forms of expression are generally considered the property of Buyer, the Covenantor's obligation is limited to items that can be conveniently located and returned.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall immediately surrender' to 'should endeavor to return,' and adding qualifiers like 'reasonably believed,' 'to the best of their understanding,' 'insofar as such items are readily accessible,' and 'conveniently located and returned,' the obligation becomes much weaker and more ambiguous. It introduces subjective interpretation and limits the scope of the return requirement. The original text creates a clear obligation to return all items, while the modified text creates a conditional and less stringent requirement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) protects trade secrets from misappropriation. By weakening the requirement to return materials containing confidential information, the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation. CUTSA allows for injunctive relief to prevent actual or threatened misappropriation. The modified language makes it harder for the Buyer to prove threatened misappropriation because the Covenantor's obligation to return materials is less clear and more discretionary. By reducing the protection of trade secrets, this alteration potentially conflicts with the intent and protections provided by CUTSA.

UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)

perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the obligation to return confidential information, potentially conflicting with the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), which aims to protect trade secrets from misappropriation. The original text's 'shall immediately surrender' becomes the much weaker 'should endeavor to return,' introducing ambiguity and discretion. The added qualifiers like 'reasonably believed,' 'to the best of their understanding,' and 'conveniently located and returned' make it harder to enforce the return of confidential information, potentially increasing the risk of misappropriation, which CUTSA addresses. While the provided snippets do not detail specific enforcement mechanisms within CUTSA, the Law Explanation suggests that weakening return obligations could hinder the Buyer's ability to prove 'threatened misappropriation,' indicating a contradiction with the intent to protect trade secrets.
